w:r.w:t: A Modern Companion to Adi Shankaracharya's 31 Verses
Timeless Wisdom for the Modern Mind

Adi Shankaracharya composed Bhaja Govindam — also known as Moha Mudgara, The Hammer on Delusion — in 8th century India. The story goes that he saw an elderly Brahmin obsessively memorising the rules of Panini's grammar, and was struck by the tragedy of a man spending his final years accumulating knowledge while the deeper questions of existence went unasked.

What Shankara composed in response was not a scholarly treatise but a song — vivid, urgent, sometimes harsh, sometimes tender. Its 31 verses dismantle, one by one, the comfortable illusions that most human lives are built upon: that wealth brings security, that relationships are permanent, that youth will last, that more knowledge equals more freedom.
But Bhaja Govindam is not pessimistic. It is clear-eyed. And clarity, however uncomfortable, is always the precondition for genuine freedom.

This book presents each of Shankara's 31 verses in three layers. First, the Sanskrit text and its transliteration. Second, a translation and explanation of the verse's classical meaning. Third — and this is the heart of this book — a reflection on how each verse speaks directly to life today: a world of relentless distraction, restless comparison, and the quiet epidemic of purposelessness.
The problems Shankara addressed have not changed. The names of the delusions have changed. This book is an attempt to hear his voice clearly across twelve centuries.
Seek Govinda. Whatever that means for you.
Na Hi Na Hi Rakshati Dukrinkarane
Bhaja govindam, bhaja govindam / Govindam bhaja moodhamate / Sampraapte sannihite kaale / Na hi na hi rakshati dukrin-karane
Seek Govinda, seek Govinda, seek Govinda, O fool! When the appointed time — death — comes, grammar rules will not save you.

Shankara opens with a thunderclap. He sees an elderly scholar obsessively memorising grammatical rules and cries out in compassion. All your intellectual achievements, credentials, and technical mastery are meaningless at the moment of death. Only devotion to the divine — to something larger than yourself — offers true refuge.

You are the scholar memorising grammar more often than you'd like to admit. You polish your resume, collect certificates, chase the next promotion, refresh your inbox for good news that never quite arrives. Shankara is not against learning — he is against mistaking the map for the territory. Your credentials are not your identity. Every skill you have mastered will one day be surpassed, replaced, or simply forgotten. What remains when the role is gone? Invest daily in something that cannot be taken from you: your inner life, your relationships, your sense of purpose. Seek your Govinda — whatever gives your life meaning beyond your job title.
The question is never what you know. The question is what remains when what you know is no longer enough.
Moodha jahihi dhanagama-trishnam / Kuru sadbuddhim manasi vitrishnam / Yallabhase nijakarmopaattam / Vittam tena vinodaya chittam
O fool, give up the thirst for accumulating wealth. Cultivate wisdom in your mind and be contented. Be happy with what you earn through your own righteous effort.

Shankara identifies the root of human suffering: the endless hunger for more money. The mind in the grip of greed is never satisfied. A thousand becomes ten thousand becomes a crore, yet the feeling of "enough" never arrives. True wisdom is knowing when you have sufficient — and finding contentment in what is honestly earned.

In an age of get-rich schemes and constant comparison, this verse is a radical act. Human beings have always measured themselves against their neighbour — someone richer, someone who "made it." That habit profits no one but feeds on your dissatisfaction. Shankara prescribes a counter-move: contentment with honest effort. This does not mean mediocrity. It means your ambition should serve your life, not consume it. Audit your wants this week. How many are genuine needs, and how many are borrowed desires — inherited from someone else's life, not your own?
Enough is never a number. It is a decision.
Naari stana-bhara naabhi-desham / Drishtvaa maagaa mohaavisham / Etan maamsavasaadi vikaaram / Manasi vichintaya vaaram vaaram
Do not be captivated by physical form — it is flesh, fat, and impermanence. Reflect on this again and again.

Shankara addresses sensory infatuation — the compulsive pull of physical attraction. He is not condemning love or beauty; he is pointing to the difference between conscious appreciation and unconscious captivity. The body is impermanent. When we mistake the physical form for the entire person, we build attachment on a foundation that must crumble.

Every age builds its own beauty-obsession machine, and ours is unusually sophisticated. Changing standards, endless comparison, the body you are told you should have instead of the one you were given. Shankara's question is simple: are you choosing consciously, or being chosen by your compulsions? This applies beyond romantic attraction — it applies to anything beautiful we grasp at. The new possession. The bigger house. The title that looks good to others. Reflect on what you chase and why. The object of desire changes across centuries; the restless wanting remains exactly the same.
What you chase always changes shape. What chases you never does.
Nalinii-dala-gata jalam-atitaralam / Tadvaj-jeevitam-atisaya-chapalam / Viddhi vyaadhy-abhimaana-grastam-lokam / Shoka-hatam-cha samastam
Life is as trembling and transient as a drop of water on a lotus leaf. Know that the world is consumed by disease, ego, and grief.

A water droplet on a lotus leaf trembles with every breath of wind, on the verge of falling at any moment. Our lives are exactly this — vivid, trembling, and brief. Shankara is not being pessimistic; he is being precise. Recognising impermanence is not despair — it is the beginning of wisdom.

Every generation is taught this lesson anew, usually by loss: everything can change overnight. Health, work, relationships, entire ways of life — gone in an illness, an accident, a single piece of news. What seemed permanent turns out never to have been. The correct response is not anxiety, but presence. The water droplet is beautiful precisely because it is temporary. Do not postpone living your actual life while waiting for the "right" moment of stability. That moment is a myth. It was always a myth.
Impermanence is not life's flaw. It is life's greatest teacher.
Yaavad-vitto-paarjana saktaha / Taavan-nija parivaro raktaha / Pashchaaj-jeevati jarjara dehe / Vaartaam ko'pi na pricchati gehe
As long as you can earn money, your family is devoted to you. When your body weakens in old age, no one in the house even asks about your welfare.

A bitter but honest observation: many relationships are transactional at their core. When you are productive and earning, you are beloved. When you are old, frail, and no longer contributing economically, the warmth fades. Shankara is not preaching cynicism — he is urging us to see clearly, and to build relationships grounded in something deeper than utility.

In an age that rewards networking and self-promotion, this verse is a scalpel. How many of your acquaintances are actually relationships? How many people in your life would show up if you lost everything tomorrow? Uncertainty in work and health keeps forcing this question into the open. Invest in genuine human bonds — not your reputation. Call the friend you have been meaning to call. Spend time with your parents while they are here. Build community, not just career. Your title will not sit beside you when you are ill.
A bond that only survives your usefulness was never a bond at all.
Yaavat pavano nivasati dehe / Taavat prichchati kushalam gehe / Gatavati vaayau dehaapaaye / Bhaaryaa bibhyati tasmin kaaye
As long as there is breath in the body, people enquire about your wellbeing. The moment the breath departs, even your spouse is afraid of the same body.

The most intimate bond — marriage — dissolves the moment life leaves the body. The person who shared your bed for decades becomes something to fear in death. Shankara uses this stark image not to terrify, but to illuminate: what is real cannot be the physical form. Something beyond the body must be cultivated if we are to live — and die — with peace.

Modern life excels at helping us avoid this truth. We medicalise death, hide it in institutions, scroll past it. But death-awareness is not morbid — it is clarifying. The Japanese practice of memento mori, the Stoic daily meditation on mortality — these are not pessimistic habits. They are the habits of people who take life seriously because they know it ends. Ask yourself today: if I had one year left, what would I stop doing? What would I start? Then start now.
Live as though the ending is real, because it is.
Baalas taavat kriidaa-saktaha / Tarunas taavat tarunii-saktaha / Vriddhas-taavat chintaa-saktaha / Parame brahmani ko'pi na saktaha
In childhood, one is attached to play; in youth, to romance; in old age, to worry. No one is ever attached to the Supreme Brahman.

Life passes in a relay of attachments, each replacing the previous. The child plays. The youth loves. The old person worries. And through it all, no one pauses to enquire into the nature of existence itself. Every stage of life is consumed by the contents of that stage — and the deeper inquiry never happens.

Replace the stages: in your twenties, you are attached to social validation. In your thirties, to career milestones. In your forties, to financial security. In your fifties, to health anxieties. The objects change; the compulsive attachment does not. And the deeper question — what is this life actually for? — never gets asked because there is always something more urgent. Shankara's invitation is not to abandon these stages but to hold them lightly. Build into your life a daily moment of the larger inquiry. Five minutes of stillness. A journal question: what truly matters? The urgent will always crowd out the important unless you protect the important.
The question that matters most is the one you keep postponing.
Kaate kaantaa kaste putraha / Samsaaro'yam atiiva vichitrah / Kasya tvam kah kuta aagatah / Tattvam chintaya tadiha bhraatah
Who is your spouse? Who is your son? This world is a strange mystery. Think on the truth: whose are you, who are you, where have you come from?

Shankara poses the three great existential questions directly: Who are you? Where did you come from? To whom do you belong? These are not questions to be answered intellectually but to be lived into. The relationships we consider most defining — spouse, child — are temporary arrangements in a vast and mysterious existence. Wisdom begins with honest bewilderment.

Everyone today has a ready answer to "who are you." You are your job title, your nationality, your affiliations, your preferences. The identity is packaged and presented to others before it is ever examined. But Shankara is pointing to the question beneath the package. Who is the one watching your thoughts? Who is the awareness behind the persona? These are not religious questions — they are psychological ones. The most grounded, resilient people in any era are those who have a stable sense of self that does not depend on external validation. Find out who you are before the world tells you.
Before you can be truly seen, you must first be found — by yourself.
Satsangatve nissangatvam / Nissangatve nirmohatvam / Nirmohatve nishchala-tatvam / Nishchalatve jivanmuktih
From good company comes non-attachment; from non-attachment comes freedom from delusion; from freedom from delusion comes steadiness of mind; from steadiness of mind comes liberation while living.

This verse is the only one in Bhaja Govindam that maps a positive path — a step-by-step progression from beginning to liberation. It starts with satsang, good company. The sequence is elegant: right company loosens unhealthy attachments; loosened attachments dissolve delusion; clarity of mind produces steadiness; steadiness is liberation itself.

This is perhaps the most actionable verse in the entire text. You become the average of who you spend time with — this is not self-help cliche, it is ancient wisdom. Your "company" today includes not just the people you sit with, but the voices you let into your mind — what you read, what you watch, who you listen to. Audit ruthlessly. Does your environment pull you toward clarity or toward anxiety? Toward wisdom or toward comparison? The sequence Shankara gives works in reverse too: bad company creates attachment, which creates delusion, which creates restlessness, which creates suffering. You are always being shaped by your environment. Choose it deliberately.
Choose your companions carefully. They quietly become your future.
Vayasi gate kah kaama-vikaarah / Shushke neere kah kaasaarah / Kshiine vitte kah parivaarah / Jnaate tattve kah samsaarah
When youth has passed, where is lust? When the lake has dried, where is it? When wealth is gone, where are the relatives? When truth is known, where is the world of illusion?

Four rhetorical questions that reveal the conditional nature of all worldly experience. Desire depends on youth; beauty depends on water; loyalty depends on wealth; the illusion of the world depends on ignorance. Remove the condition, and the experience vanishes. The only thing that does not depend on conditions is the truth itself.

Everything in your current life is conditional. Your influence depends on your relevance. Your income depends on circumstances beyond your control. Your social circle depends on proximity and shared interest. This is not a pessimistic observation — it is liberating. If you build your identity on conditional things, you will live in constant anxiety about those conditions changing. Build something unconditional: your values, your character, your capacity for presence. These survive setbacks, loss, and change. When the ground shifts beneath your work or your world, who you are at your core is what remains.
Nothing conditional was ever meant to hold your whole weight.
Maa Kuru Dhana Jana Yauvana Garvam,
Maa kuru dhana jana yauvana garvam / Harati nimeshaat kaalah sarvam / Maayaamayam idam-akhilam hitvaa / Brahmapadam tvam pravisha viditva
Do not take pride in wealth, followers, or youth. Time seizes all of these in a moment. Knowing that this entire world is illusion, enter the abode of Brahman.

Three sources of human pride — money, social standing, and youth — are named and dismissed in one sweep. Time is the great equaliser. No one escapes it. Having acknowledged what time takes, Shankara points to what time cannot touch: the ground of pure being, Brahman. To "enter" it is not to go somewhere — it is to recognise what was always already here.

Reputation, income, and physical vigour are this era's version of this trinity. Your standing, your earnings, your fitness — all will decline. The most admired person of today becomes forgotten in a few years. The most capable performer becomes replaceable when circumstances change. Shankara is not saying do not earn or do not care for your health. He is saying: do not build your dignity on these things, because they will be taken. Build your sense of worth on something that cannot be taken. Your kindness. Your integrity. Your commitment to growth. These are the currencies that compound.
Everything time can take was never safe to build your identity upon.
Dinayaaminyau saayam praatah / Shishira-vasantau punar-aayaatah / Kaalah kreedati gachchhaty-aayuh / Tadapi na munchaty-aashaa-vaayuh
Day and night, dusk and dawn, winter and spring — time plays and life ebbs away. Yet the wind of hope does not release its grip.

Time flows with indifferent consistency. The seasons cycle. Life reduces with every rotation. And yet, despite watching this happen, human beings cling to hope — not the wise hope of purposeful action, but the delusional hope that somehow our situation is the exception, that decline will spare us, that there will be time to do what matters later.

"I will do it next year" is the motto of every age. Next year I will take that course. Next year I will spend more time with my family. Next year I will write that book, start that project, have that conversation. But time does not wait for next year. The person whose industry is changing, whose parent is ageing, whose child is growing up faster than expected — none of them have a next year to prepare in. The season is already changing. Shankara's call is for urgency without panic: the urgency of someone who values their time because they know it is finite.
There is no next year. There is only this one, disguised as a rehearsal.
Dvaadasha-manjarikaa bhikshaashee / Vishnorvishaye dridha-chittee / Sankalpa-vikalpana rahitaha / Naama prabhaavanaa manasijee
One who lives on alms, who is firm in devotion to Vishnu, who is free from desires and doubts — such a one has conquered the mind born of delusion.

This verse, attributed to Shankara's disciple Hastamalaka, describes the liberated sage: one who has renounced the game of accumulation, who holds a stable inner orientation, and who lives free from the oscillation of wanting and not-wanting. Freedom from sankalpa (intention-as-craving) and vikalpa (doubt) is the mark of a settled mind.

You do not need to become a monk. But the inner quality described here is available to all: a mind that is not constantly swinging between craving and aversion, hope and fear, attraction and repulsion. This is what meditation teachers call equanimity; what psychologists call affect regulation; what Vedantins call freedom from sankalpa-vikalpa. The practical version: notice your mind's oscillations today. When you feel pulled toward something or pushed away from something, pause. Is this genuine discernment, or just the habitual swing of the pendulum? Cultivate the pause. The pause is the beginning of freedom.
Freedom is not the absence of the pull. It is the pause before you follow it.
Angam galitam palitam mundam / Dashana viheenim jaatam tundam / Vriddho yaati griheetve dandam / Tadapi na munchaty-aashaa pindam
The body has become feeble, the head is grey, the mouth has lost its teeth, the old man walks with a staff — yet still he does not release the bundle of his desires.

A vivid, almost wry image: the ancient man, body failing in every way, still clutching his desires. Shankara renders this with gentle irony. The tragedy is not that we age — it is that we age without growing wise. The capacity for desire outlasts the body's capacity to fulfil it, producing only suffering.

Look around and you will see this pattern at every age. People accumulating possessions they have no time to enjoy. Collecting experiences for the memory they will show others rather than the memory itself. Working overtime on a career that has no room left for actual living. The desire machine has no off switch built in. You have to install one yourself. The question to ask is not "how do I get more?" but "how much is enough?" Draw that line. Everything beyond it is the old man's staff — something to lean on that does not actually take you anywhere.
More is not a finish line. It is a habit with no destination.
Agre vahnih prishthe bhaanuh / Raatrau chubuka-samarpita jaanuh / Karatalab-hiksha starutala-vaasah / Tadapi na munchaty-aashaa-paasah
Fire in front, sun behind, sleeping with knees pressed to chin at night, begging food with cupped hands, sleeping under trees — yet the noose of desire does not loosen.

Even extreme ascetics — those who have voluntarily embraced physical hardship and renounced material comfort — can be trapped by subtler desires: the desire for spiritual attainment, for respect, for liberation itself. Desire is not only about money or pleasure. It can attach to anything, including the pursuit of its own elimination.

This is the deepest warning in the text, and the most applicable to self-improvement culture today. You can optimise your morning routine, meditate daily, eat clean, journal faithfully, and still be completely in the grip of desire — the desire to be the kind of person who has their life together. The productivity cult, the wellness industry, the self-help ecosystem — all can become forms of grasping. The question is whether your practices are serving genuine growth or feeding a more refined form of the same restlessness. Practice without attachment to the result of the practice.
Even the pursuit of peace can become another way of refusing it.
Kurute gangaa-saagara gamanam / Vrata-paripaalana-atha vaa daanam / Jnaana-viheenah sarva-matena / Muktim na bhajati janma-shatena
One may go on pilgrimages to the Ganga or the sea, observe fasts and give charity — but without Self-knowledge, liberation does not come even in a hundred births.

Rituals, philanthropy, and religious observance have value — but they are not the destination. Without the transformative clarity of self-knowledge, all external acts are like rearranging furniture in a burning house. Shankara insists that understanding — not performance — is the key.

This verse speaks directly to performative virtue in any era. Public pledges, visible generosity, carefully announced good deeds. None of these are wrong in themselves — but when they substitute for genuine inner transformation, they become sophisticated forms of avoidance. You can donate to ten causes and still be driven by ego, greed, and fear. Genuine change requires self-knowledge: understanding your actual motives, your actual patterns, your actual impact. The pilgrimages of our age are public statements and photographed charity. The jnana Shankara calls for is harder and more private.
A good deed announced is already half spent on the announcing.
Sukhato kriyate raamaa bhogah / Pashchaad dhanta shareere rogah / Yadyapi loke maranam sharanam / Tadapi na munchaty-aashaa-viranam
Physical pleasures are enjoyed with delight, but later they bring disease to the body. Even though death is the universal refuge, the archer of desire does not release his arrow.

Every pleasure has a cost — often paid later in the body. Yet knowing this, we pursue pleasures compulsively. And even death, which all of us know is coming, does not shake loose our attachments. Shankara images desire as an archer who never stops shooting even when the target is gone.

Lifestyle diseases driven by excess — metabolic disorders, stress-related illness, burnout, addiction — are a civilisational crisis in every affluent age, ours included. We know the sugar is harmful. We know the screen time is eroding our focus. We know the overwork is unsustainable. And we continue anyway. The information is not the problem — everyone knows what is good for them. The problem is the gap between knowing and living. This is what Shankara is addressing: the structural irrationality of human desire. The solution is not more information but a direct, visceral encounter with consequence — meditation, therapy, honest reflection with trusted people. Make the abstract cost real.
Knowing what is good for you was never the hard part. Living it is.
Artham-anartham bhaavaya nityam / Naasthi tatah sukhalesah satyam / Putraadhapi dhana bhaajaam bheetih / Sarvatra esha vihaati reetih
Reflect constantly that wealth is fraught with evil. There is not the least happiness from it in truth. Even from their own sons, the wealthy live in fear. This is how it always plays out everywhere.

Shankara is not saying money is evil — he is saying attachment to money as a source of security is self-defeating. The more wealth you accumulate, the more you have to lose, the more you fear loss, and the more suspicious you become — even of those closest to you. Wealth acquired for its own sake creates anxiety, not safety.

Beyond a threshold covering genuine needs and reasonable comfort, additional wealth adds surprisingly little to happiness, while adding considerably to complexity and anxiety. The wealthiest people you know are rarely the most at peace. Yet our systems relentlessly push the message that one more achievement, one more salary band, one more investment will finally produce peace. Notice when you are using financial goals as a proxy for emotional safety. The safety you are seeking cannot be purchased.
What you accumulate to feel safe can quietly become the thing you fear losing most.
Praanaayannam bhajana-naama sankhyaa / Vedaanta vakya manana nishthaa / Brahmaaham iti vibhaavanaa shuddha / Bhavati yatah pungasya muktir-niyataa
Regulated breath, chanting of divine names, contemplation of Vedantic truths, and the pure realisation of "I am Brahman" — from these, liberation is certain.

Having spent most of the text dismantling false refuges, Shankara now offers the positive prescription. Four practices: pranayama (breath regulation), nama japa (repetitive chanting), vedantic contemplation, and the living recognition of one's identity with the absolute. Together, these form an integral path — body, speech, mind, and pure awareness.

Breathwork, meditation, study, and self-inquiry are not ancient luxuries — they are survival tools in a distracted world. Breathwork (pranayama) works quickly and viscerally to shift your inner state — you do not need a study to confirm this, only your next few breaths. Repetitive practice (japa) is what athletes and musicians call deliberate practice. Contemplation of large ideas keeps you oriented toward meaning. And the recognition that you are more than your thoughts and roles — this is the inner stability that no disruption, personal or economic, can take. Begin with five minutes of slow breathing. Today.
The breath you take right now is already the beginning of the practice.
Guru-charana-ambuja nirbhara bhaktah / Samsaaraat achiraad-bhava muktah / Sendriya maanasa niyamaa-devam / Drakshyasi nija-hridaya-stham devam
One who is deeply devoted to the Guru will soon be free from worldly existence. Through disciplined senses and mind, one will see the divine dwelling in one's own heart.

The Guru is central to Vedantic tradition not as an object of blind worship but as a living pointer toward truth. The Guru accelerates the path by providing what no book can: a living example, direct correction, and the transmission of understood experience. Combined with sense-discipline and mental steadiness, one discovers that the divine is not distant — it is the very heart of one's own being.

Today, everyone seems to have an audience and a following, offering themselves as a guide. Shankara is pointing to something different: a genuine mentor relationship — someone who has walked the path you are on, who can see your blind spots because they have navigated their own. This is more valuable than any credential or course. Find your Govindapaada — in your profession, in your inner life, in your creative work. And be willing to be genuinely taught, not just validated. The second half of this verse is the prize: once the mind is disciplined and the senses are regulated, you will encounter the divine not in a temple but in your own awareness.
A guide worth following is one who, in time, makes you need them less.
Na Stree Na Naree Na Yauvana Lobhah,
Na Me Manye Kaschit Tatra Shubhah
Na Me Dveshah Na Me Raagah,
Na stree na naree na yauvana lobhah / Na me manye kaschit tatra shubhah / Na me dveshah na me raagah / Brahmaadau na me moho lagnam
I am neither man nor woman, neither youth nor the passion for it. I have no hatred, no attachment. I am not bound by delusion regarding Brahman or anything else.

This verse moves into the voice of the liberated being. It is spoken from the standpoint of pure awareness, which is beyond gender, age, preference, and identity. It is not a denial of these categories but a recognition that one's deepest nature transcends them.

Identity is a great obsession of our age — and rightly so, because the erasure of identities has caused immense historical harm. But Shankara points to a layer beneath identity: the awareness in which all identities arise. You are a woman, a citizen of some country, a professional, a parent — and you are also the awareness that knows all these designations. The latter cannot be threatened, dismissed, or made redundant. When you are grounded in that deeper layer, you can hold your social identities with lightness — advocating fiercely for justice while not being existentially destabilised when your identity is challenged.
You are not the label. You are the one who notices it was given to you.
Rathyaa chapara vibhushita vesho / Harati na chitham prabhoh sandesho / Maanas sanaatana paaravaare / Paribhramati kila taraanga bhaare
Clothed in rags or adorned in finery, the Lord's message stirs the heart. The mind wanders on the timeless ocean, amid the weight of waves.

Whether a person is dressed in a beggar's rags or royal finery, the call of the divine touches all equally. The external condition is irrelevant to inner receptivity. But the mind, like a boat on a vast ocean, is constantly tossed by waves of thought and emotion — and so even when the call comes, it is often drowned out.

The divine call — the call to your deeper self, your authentic purpose, your most meaningful work — is speaking to you regardless of your circumstances. It does not require you to be rich, successful, or spiritually advanced. But you will not hear it if your mind is in perpetual agitation. The ocean of distraction in any busy life — obligations, worries, chatter, comparison — generates waves that drown the signal. Stillness is not a luxury; it is the prerequisite for hearing what matters. Create space in your day where the waves can settle.
The call was never silent. Only the mind was too loud to hear it.
Kaama krodho lobho mohah / Tesham tyaktam dheeva bhavaah / Atma jnaana vina moksho / Naasti kimapi hi satyam
Lust, anger, greed, and delusion — give them up. These are the forest of enemies. Without self-knowledge, there is truly no liberation.

The four classical obstacles — kama (desire), krodha (anger), lobha (greed), moha (delusion) — are explicitly named as the enemies of freedom. The solution is not mere suppression but self-knowledge. You do not escape these forces by willing them away. You transcend them through understanding their nature and your own.

These four are not abstract sins — they show up wherever human attention can be captured. Desire is stirred by what promises pleasure or status. Anger is stirred by what threatens us or offends our sense of fairness. Greed is stirred by scarcity, real or manufactured. Delusion is stirred by whatever confirms what we already believe. You are not weak for being affected — you are human, and these forces have always existed, sharpened further in every age that learns to exploit them. But Shankara's insight holds: the antidote is not willpower but understanding. When you understand how these mechanisms work — in the world around you and in yourself — you gain the freedom to choose differently.
What captures your attention today will, in time, come to own it.
Geyam gitaa naama sahasram / Dhyeyam shree-pati roopam ajasram / Neyam sajjana sange chittam / Deyam daaridraya eva vittam
Chant the Gita and the thousand names of the Lord. Meditate always on the form of the divine. Guide the mind toward the company of the good. Give your wealth to the needy.

A four-fold practical programme: scriptural engagement, meditation, good company, and generous giving. This is Shankara's positive prescription after all the warnings — not a list of prohibitions but a vision of a well-lived life. Notably, charity is included not as a moral obligation but as a natural overflow of a wisdom-oriented life.

Translate this for today: read something meaningful, not just useful. Meditate — even five minutes daily. Curate your company toward wisdom and genuine goodness. And give — money, time, skill — to those with less. Every honest account of a flourishing life keeps circling back to some version of these four elements: meaningful engagement, contemplative practice, community, and contribution. Shankara arrived at the same formula twelve centuries ago, simply by paying close attention. These are not religious recommendations; they are perennial truths about human thriving.
A well-lived life is rarely complicated. It is simply rarely practised.
Sukhapath cheth kaschit smarte vishnum / Sthaanaa sthaanaat prarthayate krishnam / Smritvaa smritvaa bhajati bhaktim / Manasaa vaachaa karma muktim
If even by chance one remembers Vishnu with ease, seeking Krishna in every place — through constant remembrance and devotion in thought, word, and deed — liberation comes.

Even accidental, casual remembrance of the divine has transformative power. Shankara does not set a perfectionist bar — even an imperfect, occasional turning toward the sacred is valued. The cultivation of this remembrance across thought, speech, and action is the full path of devotion.

You do not need to be a perfect meditator, a consistent journaller, a flawlessly disciplined person to begin. Even a moment of genuine presence — a breath taken consciously, a pause before you react, a moment of gratitude — is the beginning of transformation. The perfectionist mindset that says "I will begin my practice when I am ready" is the mind's cleverest avoidance strategy. Begin now, imperfectly. Remember your values mid-conversation. Pause before you send the angry message. Look up from the screen for a moment. These small remembrances add up.
You do not need to be ready. You only need to begin.
Praanaayaama pratyaahaara / Nityaanitya viveka-vichaara / Jaapyasameta samaadhi-bhaasam / Kurvavadhaanam mahad-iha maanas
Practise pranayama and pratyahara — withdrawal of senses. Engage in discrimination between the eternal and the transient. Along with japa, practise samadhi. Give great attention to the mind.

A technical prescription from the yogic tradition: breath regulation, sense withdrawal, discriminative reasoning, repetitive practice, and states of meditative absorption. The instruction to "give great attention to the mind" is the central teaching — the mind itself must become the object of study.

This verse describes a practice, not a theory, and its effects are easy to notice for yourself. Breathwork steadies the body. Sense withdrawal — deliberately limiting stimulation — restores your capacity to think clearly. Discriminative reasoning is what we now call critical thinking. Japa is deliberate practice. And samadhi — absorbed concentration — is what we now call flow. The mind, the verse insists, must receive your great attention. Yet most people give their mind to anyone who wants it — advertisers, algorithms, newsfeeds — and wonder why they feel scattered and anxious. Take your mind back.
Whoever holds your attention is quietly shaping who you become.
Guravam praaptam idam vacho / Saaram imam bhaja govindam / Yo jnaatva atmanam svaatmanam / Sa mukto bhavati naatra samshayah
These words have come from the Guru's wisdom. The essence is: seek Govinda. One who knows the Self by the Self is liberated — there is no doubt about this.

Shankara returns to the refrain — Bhaja Govindam — now framing it as the essence of all teaching. After all the analysis, all the dismantling of illusions, all the practical prescriptions, the message resolves into this simple imperative. And the liberating truth is stated without qualification: self-knowledge is freedom.

All the complexity of self-help, career strategy, relationships, and grief eventually collapses into one question: do you know who you are? Not your credentials, your achievements, or your reputation — but the awareness behind all of these. People who know themselves clearly — their values, their patterns, their actual strengths and actual weaknesses — navigate loss, change, and uncertainty far better than those who have only external anchors. Know yourself. That knowledge is the one thing that cannot be taken from you.
Every external anchor eventually gives way. The one that does not is the one you build within.
Bhaja govindam bhaja govindam / Govindam bhaja moodhamate / Naama-smarat kimanyat / Mokshasyaanaadir-aahitam
Seek Govinda, seek Govinda, seek Govinda, O fool! What else can save you? The path to liberation has always been through the remembrance of the divine name.

The refrain returns again. But by now, its meaning has deepened through all that came before. "Govinda" is no longer just a name of Krishna — it is a pointer to the ground of being, to what is real and enduring in an impermanent world. The fool who is addressed is each of us, in our moments of forgetting.

In your most distracted, anxious, overwhelmed moment — when the demands are crushing, the news is bleak, grief or uncertainty is paralysing — what do you return to? What is your Govinda? It does not have to be a deity. It can be a value: kindness. A practice: breath. A person: your child's face, a parent's voice. A question: what actually matters? Find your anchor and return to it. Not once, but again and again. The practice is the return, not the perfection of the staying.
In the moment you forget yourself, the practice is simply to remember — again.
Sura-mandira taru-muula nivaasah / Shayyaa bhootalamajina vaasah / Sarva parigraha bhoga tyaagah / Kasya sukham na karoti viraagah
Living under temple eaves or at the roots of trees, sleeping on the ground, having renounced all possession and pleasure — does not such dispassion bring joy to all?

A question about radical simplicity. The sage who has given up everything — comfort, possession, social status — and lives in the most basic conditions: is he not at peace? Shankara points to the paradox that the grasping after comfort often produces more discomfort than the embrace of simplicity.

The pull toward simpler living — fewer possessions, quieter days, deliberate withdrawal from noise — is a modern approximation of what this verse describes, and every generation rediscovers it in its own language. People who have reached peak consumption are discovering what Shankara knew: above a threshold, more stuff creates more noise, not more peace. This is not a call to live under a tree. But it is a call to examine how much of your life energy is spent maintaining possessions, obligations, and appearances that do not serve your wellbeing. Simplify deliberately. Subtract what is not adding. The spaciousness that results is not poverty — it is clarity.
What you let go of makes room for what was already enough.
Yogarato vaa bhogarato vaa / Sangarato vaa sanga-viheenah / Yasya brahmaani ramate chittam / Nandati nandati nandatyeva
Whether engaged in yoga or in pleasure, whether in company or in solitude — one whose mind delights in Brahman truly rejoices, rejoices, rejoices.

The penultimate verse reveals the great secret: liberation is not about what you do but about the quality of awareness you bring. Whether you are a yogi or a householder, in community or in solitude, active or contemplative — if your mind rests in the ground of pure being, joy is the natural result. The three-fold repetition of "rejoices" is Shankara's exclamation of wonder.

This is the most liberating verse in the text. It means the path is open to everyone — the busy parent, the working professional, the artist, the monk. You do not need a different life to begin. You need a different quality of presence within your current life. The lawyer who practises with full awareness, the teacher who shows up with genuine attention, the parent who is truly present at dinner — when grounded in the larger awareness, these are already rejoicing. Bring your whole self to wherever you already are.
Joy is not found in a different life. It is found in a different way of living this one.
Bhaja govindam bhaja govindam / Govindam bhaja moodhamate / Janma janmaantara sanchita-paapam / Smarana ekena nashyati
Seek Govinda, seek Govinda, seek Govinda, O fool! The accumulated sins of many births are destroyed by a single act of sincere remembrance.

The final verse closes where the first opened — with the refrain. Its ending carries extraordinary grace: even lifetimes of accumulated wrong action, confusion, and harm can be dissolved by a single sincere act of turning toward what is real. This is not a promise of magical escape from consequences — it is an affirmation that the door of transformation is always open, always now, regardless of how far one has strayed.

No matter how long you have been distracted, how many years you have spent running from what matters, how many choices you regret — the return is always available. Now. This moment. There is no accumulated deficit of wrong living that bars you from beginning again. The first sincere breath taken in awareness is your Govinda. The first moment of genuine presence after years of absence is your beginning. Shankara ends his great song not with a threat but with an open door. Step through it.
It is never too late to begin again, because beginning again is the whole practice.

Bhaja Govindam ends where it begins. The refrain does not resolve — it returns. This is intentional. Liberation, in Shankara's vision, is not a destination reached once and left behind. It is a practice of return: again and again, to the ground of your own being, to what is real beneath the noise of wanting and fearing.

The 31 verses you have just passed through are not 31 different teachings. They are 31 angles on a single teaching: wake up. See clearly. Stop mistaking the map for the territory. And seek — with whatever you have, from wherever you are — the Govinda that is the ground of your own existence.

This is not spiritual advice. It is practical wisdom, as true now as it was twelve centuries ago. The disruptions of any era — economic, political, personal, mortal — will separate those who know who they are from those who only know what they do. The first group will navigate; the second will be navigated.
Shankara's fool is not unintelligent. He is someone who has confused accumulation for arrival, performance for purpose, information for understanding. We are all, at times, that fool.
The invitation is always open. Begin again.
Bhaja Govindam. Bhaja Govindam. Govindam Bhaja.
If this book helped you think differently, you may also enjoy other Timeless Companions from ThinkSriram — modern-day walks alongside the texts that have guided thoughtful people for centuries:
The Stoics and Socrates, read for a life lived today.
The Bhagavad Gita for Modern Life
Krishna's counsel to Arjuna, and to you.
Marcus Aurelius, one honest page at a time.
